The 3x leveraged long semiconductor ETF, $SOXL, posted +$1.2 billion in inflows, the 2nd-largest daily inflow this year.

In total, US long semiconductor ETFs took in +$7.1 billion in fresh capital on Tuesday.

Year-to-date, $SOXX alone has posted +$13.3 billion in inflows.

Investors are buying the dip in semiconductors at an unprecedented pace.

At the same time, the proportion of the 250 smallest companies has halved, to ~7%, the lowest since at least 2014.

To put this differently, the top 10 now account for more than 6 times the market cap of the smallest 250 firms in the S&P 500 index.

A handful of stocks continue to effectively drive the entire market.

thread_tweets.text:
Wow, keep doubling down on the misleading numbers. Bold!

You know that the $40B denominator includes land (no depreciation, no maintenance capex), assets under construction (future depreciation, zero current maintenance capex), and JV gross-ups where DLR bears only its pro-rata share. 

On 2,100 MW of in-service, fully operational capacity, $300M/yr = $143K/MW/yr. That's the right denominator for maintenance capex 

Hindenburg's point actually helps here as they argued DLR understates recurring capex because of tenant churn and IT responsibility. 

WULF has no churn for 20 years and no IT responsibility per the 8-K. 

The critique you're citing is an argument against DLR's business model, not WULF's.
